safety.pageSubtitle: Emergency contacts and step-by-step guidance for before, during, and after a disaster.
safety.disclaimer: Guidance adapted from FEMA, Ready.gov, and the American Red Cross. Always follow instructions from your local emergency officials.

zip.label: Find local help by zipcode
zip.invalid: Please enter a valid 5-digit US zipcode.
zip.lookupError: Could not look up that zipcode. Please try again.

before.0: Know your evacuation zone and route.
before.1: Build a kit: 3 days of water (1 gal/person/day), non-perishable food, meds, flashlight, batteries.
before.2: Keep documents (ID, insurance, medical) in a waterproof bag.
before.3: Charge phones and backup batteries; fill your car with gas.

during.0: Evacuate immediately if told to. Never drive through flood water — “Turn Around, Don’t Drown.”
during.1: Move to the highest level of the building; avoid basements.
during.2: Stay away from windows; shelter in an interior room.
during.3: Listen to local officials on a battery or hand-crank radio.

after.0: Return home only when authorities say it’s safe.
after.1: Avoid standing water — it may be electrically charged or contaminated.
after.2: Photograph damage for insurance before cleaning up.
after.3: Check on neighbors, especially older adults and people with disabilities.

before.0: Clear leaves, brush, and debris within 30 ft of your home.
before.1: Pack a go-bag and keep it by the door.
before.2: Sign up for local emergency alerts.
before.3: Plan two escape routes out of your neighborhood.

during.0: Leave early — don’t wait for an evacuation order if you feel unsafe.
during.1: Wear an N95 mask; close all windows and doors before leaving.
during.2: If trapped, call 911 and shelter in a cleared area away from vegetation.
during.3: Keep headlights on; drive slowly through smoke.

after.0: Wait for the “all clear” before returning.
after.1: Watch for hot spots, ash pits, and downed power lines.
after.2: Wear gloves and a mask during cleanup; ash can be toxic.
after.3: Document losses for insurance and FEMA claims.

before.0: Secure heavy furniture, water heaters, and shelves to walls.
before.1: Identify safe spots: under sturdy tables, against interior walls.
before.2: Keep sturdy shoes and a flashlight by your bed.
before.3: Store an emergency kit and know how to shut off gas and water.

during.0: Drop, Cover, and Hold On — get under sturdy furniture.
during.1: Stay indoors until shaking stops; don’t run outside.
during.2: If outdoors, move to an open area away from buildings and wires.
during.3: If driving, pull over away from overpasses and stop.

after.0: Expect aftershocks; Drop, Cover, and Hold On again if they come.
after.1: Check for injuries and gas leaks — leave if you smell gas.
after.2: Use texts, not calls, to keep phone lines open.
after.3: Inspect your home for damage before re-entering.

before.0: Know the difference: a Watch means possible; a Warning means take shelter now.
before.1: Identify a safe room — basement or an interior room on the lowest floor.
before.2: Keep a helmet or heavy blankets to protect your head.
before.3: Enable Wireless Emergency Alerts on your phone.

during.0: Go to your safe room immediately; put as many walls between you and outside as possible.
during.1: Cover your head and neck; crouch low.
during.2: Avoid windows, doors, and outside walls.
during.3: If in a mobile home or car, get to a sturdy building.

after.0: Watch for broken glass, nails, and downed power lines.
after.1: Don’t enter damaged buildings until they’re inspected.
after.2: Help injured people but don’t move the seriously hurt unless in danger.
after.3: Take photos of damage for your insurance.

before.0: Insulate pipes and know how to shut off water if they freeze.
before.1: Stock extra blankets, warm clothing, food, and water.
before.2: Keep flashlights and batteries ready for outages.
before.3: Service heating equipment and check smoke/CO detectors.

during.0: Stay indoors and dress in layers.
during.1: Never use a generator, grill, or camp stove indoors — carbon monoxide risk.
during.2: Keep faucets dripping slightly to prevent frozen pipes.
during.3: If you lose heat, close off unused rooms to conserve warmth.

after.0: Check on neighbors at risk from the cold.
after.1: Watch for signs of frostbite and hypothermia.
after.2: Clear snow from vents and exhausts to avoid CO buildup.

before.0: Identify air-conditioned public places (libraries, malls, cooling centers).
before.1: Check that fans and AC work; cover windows that get morning/afternoon sun.
before.2: Stock extra water and electrolyte drinks.
before.3: Plan to check on older or isolated neighbors.

during.0: Drink water often — don’t wait until you’re thirsty.
during.1: Stay in the coolest part of your home or a cooling center.
during.2: Never leave people or pets in a parked car.
during.3: Limit outdoor activity to early morning or evening.

after.0: Watch for heat exhaustion (heavy sweating, weakness) and heat stroke (confusion, hot dry skin — call 911).
after.1: Keep hydrating and resting in a cool place.
after.2: Check on family, neighbors, and pets.
after.3: Restock your water and supplies for the next wave.
